body_md: Summary: Tradition (Eusebius) holds Mark founded the Alexandrian church c.42 AD; the claim is ancient but first attested in 2nd-century sources, not contemporary. Uncertainty: no 1st-century documentation of Alexandrian church. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 2.16. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250102.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:mark-the-evangelist]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:jewish-christian]] [[persuasion:miaphysite]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Paul's Galatian letter (c.48-55 AD) addresses churches in Galatia; Ancyra as the provincial capital is a plausible early contact point. Uncertainty: Galatian letter's recipients debated between N. and S. Galatia. Evidence: Galatians 1:2; Acts 16:6. Citations: https://biblehub.com/galatians/1-2.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:unnamed-missionaries]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Disciples first called 'Christians' at Antioch (Acts 11:26); Barnabas arrives from Jerusalem, then summons Paul from Tarsus. Uncertainty: tradition of Petrine co-founding is strong but secondary. Evidence: Acts 11:19-26. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/11-26.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:barnabas]] [[person:paul]] [[person:peter]] [[person:silas]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:jewish-christian]] [[persuasion:miaphysite]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Ascalon (Ashkelon) is one of the five Philistine cities of the coastal plain, situated between Gaza and Joppa; Philip the Evangelist evangelizes the coastal plain between Azotus and Caesarea (Acts 8:40), and Ascalon lies along this route; Christian presence probable from the early mission. Evidence: Acts 8:39-40; general inference. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/8-40.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:unnamed-local-community]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Paul and Barnabas preach the gospel at Attalia (Acts 14:25) and then sail from there back to Antioch (Acts 14:26), completing Paul's first missionary journey. Attalia is the main port of Pamphylia. Evidence: Acts 14:25-26. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/14-25.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:barnabas]] [[person:paul]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: The Spirit of the Lord 'snatches Philip away' from the road to Gaza after baptizing the Ethiopian eunuch and 'Philip found himself at Azotus (Ashdod)'; he then evangelizes all the cities from Azotus northward to Caesarea Maritima (Acts 8:39-40). Evidence: Acts 8:39-40. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/8-39.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:philip-the-evangelist]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Berytus (modern Beirut) is a major Roman port city in Phoenicia; Christian presence probable given its position on the coastal trade route between Antioch and Alexandria. It is known for its famous Roman law school. Uncertainty: no direct NT reference to Berytus. Evidence: General inference. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/place/Beirut\n\nMentions: [[person:unnamed-missionaries]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Bethany is the home of Mary, Martha, and Lazarus — Jesus's closest friends outside the Twelve. Jesus raises Lazarus from the dead here (John 11:1-44), his most dramatic miracle before the crucifixion; he is also anointed here (Matt 26:6-13; John 12:1-8); and the ascension tradition is associated with the road from Bethany to the Mount of Olives (Luke 24:50-51). Evidence: John 11:1-44; Luke 24:50-51. Citations: https://biblehub.com/john/11-1.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:jesus]] [[person:lazarus]] [[person:martha]] [[person:mary]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Bethlehem is Jesus's birthplace (Matt 2:1; Luke 2:4-7); wise men visit (Matt 2:1-12); the slaughter of the innocents occurs under Herod. An early Jewish Christian community maintains the memory of these events. Evidence: Matt 2:1; Luke 2:4-7. Citations: https://biblehub.com/matthew/2-1.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:unnamed-local-community]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Brundisium (Brindisi) is the terminus of the Appian Way and the main Roman port for crossing to Greece and the East; Christian presence probable from the earliest period as all travelers between Rome and the East pass through here. Evidence: General inference. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/place/Brindisi\n\nMentions: [[person:unnamed-local-community]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Philip the Evangelist settles in Caesarea (Acts 21:8); Peter's baptism of Cornelius here (~40 AD) is a pivotal Gentile admission event. Evidence: Acts 10; Acts 21:8. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/10.htm ; https://biblehub.com/acts/21-8.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:cornelius]] [[person:peter]] [[person:philip-the-evangelist]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:jewish-christian]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: At Caesarea Philippi, Jesus asks 'Who do men say that I am?' and Peter confesses 'You are the Christ, the Son of the living God' (Matt 16:13-20) — the pivotal moment of Peter's leadership commission and the promise to build the Church on this rock. Evidence: Matt 16:13-20; Mark 8:27-30; Luke 9:18-20. Citations: https://biblehub.com/matthew/16-13.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:jesus]] [[person:peter]] [[person:the-twelve]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Capernaum is Jesus's ministry base ('his own city', Matt 9:1; 4:13); here he heals Peter's mother-in-law (Matt 8:14), the centurion's servant (Matt 8:5-13), the paralytic (Mark 2:1-12), and many others; he teaches in the synagogue (John 6:59); he calls Matthew/Levi (Matt 9:9). Peter and Andrew live here. Evidence: Matt 4:13; 9:1; Mark 2:1; John 6:59. Citations: https://biblehub.com/matthew/4-13.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:andrew]] [[person:james]] [[person:jesus]] [[person:john]] [[person:levi-matthew]] [[person:peter]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Capua (modern Santa Maria Capua Vetere) is the second-most important city of Roman Italy after Rome, located on the Appian Way; Christian presence probable by mid-1st century given its proximity to Puteoli (where Paul landed, Acts 28:13) and constant traffic to and from Rome. Evidence: General inference. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/place/Capua\n\nMentions: [[person:unnamed-local-community]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Carrhae (Harran, modern Turkey) is the ancient city where Abraham sojourned (Gen 11:31-12:4) on the way from Ur to Canaan; as part of the Edessa network it likely has early Christian presence, and later becomes an important Syriac Christian city. Evidence: General inference; Gen 11:31. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/place/Harran\n\nMentions: [[person:unnamed-missionaries]] [[persuasion:church-of-the-east]] [[persuasion:miaphysite]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:parthian-roman-border]]

Summary: Paul and Barnabas begin first missionary journey at Salamis, Cyprus (Acts 13:4-12); proconsul Sergius Paulus believes. Barnabas was a native Cypriot (Acts 4:36). Evidence: Acts 13:4-12. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/13-4.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:barnabas]] [[person:john-mark]] [[person:paul]] [[person:sergius-paulus]] [[persuasion:jewish-christian]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Simon of Cyrene carries Jesus' cross (Mark 15:21); Cyrenean Jews witness Pentecost (Acts 2:10); Cyrenaean members of the Freedmen's synagogue in Jerusalem dispute with Stephen (Acts 6:9). Men from Cyrene plant the church in Antioch (Acts 11:20). Evidence: Acts 2:10; 6:9; 11:20; Mark 15:21. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/11-20.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:lucius-of-cyrene]] [[person:unnamed-brothers]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Paul's conversion on road to Damascus (~33-35 AD) and baptism by Ananias; a Christian community pre-existed Paul's arrival. Evidence: Acts 9:1-22; Gal 1:17. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/9.htm ; https://biblehub.com/galatians/1-17.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:ananias-of-damascus]] [[person:paul]] [[persuasion:jewish-christian]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: The Abgar Legend holds that King Abgar V of Edessa wrote to Jesus requesting healing and received a reply letter; after Jesus's death, the apostle Thaddaeus (Addai) is said to have come to heal and evangelize the king. Eusebius claims to have found the original Aramaic correspondence in the Edessene royal archives (Ecc. Hist. 1.13). The historical core is uncertain but Edessa has one of the oldest attested Christian communities east of the Euphrates. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 1.13; Doctrina Addai. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250101.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:king-abgar-v]] [[person:thaddaeus]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:church-of-the-east]] [[persuasion:jewish-christian]] [[persuasion:miaphysite]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:parthian-roman-border]]

Summary: On the day of the Resurrection, the risen Jesus walks unrecognized with two disciples to Emmaus (~7 miles from Jerusalem), expounds the scriptures, then is recognized at the breaking of bread (Luke 24:13-35) — one of the most theologically rich resurrection appearances. The exact site is disputed between Latrun, Qubeibeh, Abu Ghosh, and Motza. Evidence: Luke 24:13-35. Citations: https://biblehub.com/luke/24-13.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:cleopas]] [[person:jesus]] [[person:unnamed-disciple]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Philip the Evangelist preaches in Samaria (Acts 8:5-25) and Peter and John visit to confirm the conversions; Flavia Neapolis (modern Nablus, near ancient Shechem) is the main Samaritan city. Christian presence probable from the earliest Samaritan mission. Evidence: Acts 8:5-25. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/8-5.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:philip-the-evangelist]] [[person:unnamed-samaritan-converts]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:jewish-christian]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Jesus crosses the Sea of Galilee to Gadara (Gerasa in some MSS) and heals two men possessed by demons ('Legion'); the demons enter a herd of pigs that rush into the sea (Matt 8:28-34; Mark 5:1-20; Luke 8:26-39); the healed man becomes a missionary to the Decapolis ('Go tell what God has done for you' — Mark 5:19-20). Evidence: Matt 8:28-34; Mark 5:1-20. Citations: https://biblehub.com/mark/5-1.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:jesus]] [[person:unnamed-freed-demoniacs]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:jewish-christian]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Philip the Evangelist is directed by an angel to the road from Jerusalem to Gaza (Acts 8:26) where he encounters and baptizes the Ethiopian eunuch, a court official of the Kandake of Ethiopia; Philip is then transported to Azotus and preaches north to Caesarea. Evidence: Acts 8:26-40. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/8-26.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:philip-the-evangelist]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Paul leaves Titus in Crete to 'set in order what remains and appoint elders in every city' (Titus 1:5), implying multiple Christian communities across the island by ~57 AD. Gortyna is the provincial capital of Creta et Cyrenaica. Paul's ship stops at Fair Havens in Crete (Acts 27:8-12). Evidence: Titus 1:5; Acts 27:8-12. Citations: https://biblehub.com/titus/1-5.htm ; https://biblehub.com/acts/27-8.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:paul]] [[person:titus]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Jerusalem is founding locus; James the Just leads as first bishop; Acts 2-12 attest continuous community from Pentecost. Uncertainty: dates of individual apostolic departures vary. Evidence: Acts 2-12; Gal 1:18-19. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/2.htm ; https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2501.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:james-the-just]] [[person:john]] [[person:peter]] [[person:philip-the-evangelist]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:jewish-christian]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Peter raises Tabitha/Dorcas from the dead at Joppa and stays with Simon the Tanner; sees the vision of clean/unclean animals leading to Cornelius baptism. Evidence: Acts 9:36-43; 10:5-23. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/9-36.htm

---

Summary: Joppa is the NT's most pivotal coastal city for the Gentile mission: Peter raises Tabitha (Dorcas) here (Acts 9:36-43) and while staying at Simon the Tanner's house receives the vision of clean/unclean animals (Acts 10:9-16) that leads him to baptize the Gentile centurion Cornelius in Caesarea — the theological break-through for the Gentile church. Evidence: Acts 9:36-43; Acts 10:1-48. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/9-36.htm ; https://biblehub.com/acts/10-9.htm

Mentions: [[person:peter]] [[person:simon-the-tanner]] [[person:tabitha]] [[person:tabitha-dorcas]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:jewish-christian]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Laodicea ad Mare (modern Latakia) is a major Syrian port city and one of the four cities of the Tetrapolis; NOT to be confused with Laodicea in Phrygia (Colossians, Revelation). Christian presence probable from early missions spreading from Antioch. Evidence: General inference. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/cathen/08756a.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:unnamed-missionaries]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Memphis (biblical Noph/Moph; modern Mit Rahina) is the ancient capital of Egypt; the Christian tradition holds that the Holy Family passed through the Memphis area during the Flight to Egypt (Matt 2:13-15), and multiple Coptic traditions locate holy sites along this route. Christian community probable by mid-1st century given Alexandria's proximity and the large Jewish diaspora population. Evidence: Matt 2:13-15; Coptic tradition. Citations: https://biblehub.com/matthew/2-13.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:holy-family]] [[person:unnamed-community]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:jewish-christian]] [[persuasion:miaphysite]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Nazareth is Jesus's hometown where he was raised (Matt 2:23; Luke 4:16; 'Jesus of Nazareth'); he reads from Isaiah in the synagogue and is rejected (Luke 4:16-30); his family lives here. An early Jewish Christian community persists in Nazareth into the 3rd century. Evidence: Luke 4:16-30; Matt 2:23. Citations: https://biblehub.com/luke/4-16.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:jesus]] [[person:joseph]] [[person:mary]] [[person:unnamed-brothers-and-sisters]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Neapolis (Naples) is one of the oldest and largest Greek cities in Italy, located on the Bay of Naples adjacent to Puteoli where Paul disembarks (Acts 28:13); Christian presence probable by mid-1st century given its proximity to the Puteoli landing and constant traffic with Rome. Evidence: General inference. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/place/Naples\n\nMentions: [[person:unnamed-local-community]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Ostia is Rome's principal seaport and the gateway through which Paul would have passed en route to Rome (c.60 AD; cf. Acts 28:13-16 — Paul arrives via Puteoli but Ostia is the main port gateway); early Christian community probable given the constant flow of travelers and merchants from the East. Evidence: General inference. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/place/Ostia\n\nMentions: [[person:unnamed-local-community]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Paul and Barnabas travel across Cyprus from Salamis to Paphos (Acts 13:6-12); at Paphos they encounter Bar-Jesus/Elymas (a Jewish magician and false prophet), whom Paul strikes blind. The proconsul Sergius Paulus believes, making him one of the first high Roman officials to convert to Christianity. Evidence: Acts 13:6-12. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/13-6.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:barnabas]] [[person:paul]] [[person:sergius-paulus]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: According to Eusebius (Ecc. Hist. 3.5.3), the Jerusalem Christian community fled to Pella in the Decapolis before the Roman siege of Jerusalem (66-70 AD), heeding Jesus's warning to flee (Matt 24:16; Luke 21:21). Uncertainty: Pella flight tradition is accepted by some scholars and questioned by others. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.5.3. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250103.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:jerusalem-refugee-community]] [[persuasion:jewish-christian]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Paul and Barnabas arrive at Perga in Pamphylia (Acts 13:13); John Mark leaves here and returns to Jerusalem — a departure that will later cause Paul and Barnabas to separate (Acts 15:36-41). Paul preaches the gospel in Perga on the return journey (Acts 14:25). Evidence: Acts 13:13-14; 14:25. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/13-13.htm ; https://biblehub.com/acts/14-25.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:barnabas]] [[person:john-mark]] [[person:paul]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Paul states he went to 'Arabia' after his conversion (Gal 1:17), which many scholars interpret as the Nabataean kingdom with Petra as capital; no independent attestation of a church in Petra at this date. Uncertainty: 'Arabia' in Gal 1:17 is debated; it could refer to the Syrian desert region. Evidence: Galatians 1:17. Citations: https://biblehub.com/galatians/1-17.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:paul-of-tarsus]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:nabataean-kingdom]]

Summary: Philadelphia of Arabia (modern Amman, Jordan) is a major Decapolis city — NOT the Lydian Philadelphia of Revelation 3:7. Christian presence probable given its proximity to Pella and the Jerusalem mission networks. Evidence: General inference. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/place/Amman\n\nMentions: [[person:unnamed-missionaries]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Paul and Barnabas preach in the synagogue at Pisidian Antioch (Acts 13:14-52) on the first missionary journey; Paul's synagogue sermon here is the longest recorded in Acts; they are expelled by local authorities but make many converts. Evidence: Acts 13:14-52. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/13-14.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:barnabas]] [[person:paul]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: A Roman church existed before Paul's letter (~57 AD); tradition holds Peter arrived ~42 AD but this is debated. Claudius expelled Jewish Christians (~49 AD; Suetonius). Evidence: Romans 1:8-15; Acts 18:2; Suetonius, Claudius 25.4. Citations: https://biblehub.com/romans/1.htm ; https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2501.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:aquila]] [[person:peter]] [[person:priscilla]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:jewish-christian]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Paul states he has preached the gospel 'all the way from Jerusalem to Illyricum' (Romans 15:19); Salona is the capital of Dalmatia (Illyricum), making it the most likely center of any Pauline mission to the region. Uncertainty: Paul mentions the region but not Salona specifically. Evidence: Romans 15:19. Citations: https://biblehub.com/romans/15-19.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:unnamed-missionaries]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Philip the Evangelist preaches in Samaria (Acts 8:4-25); Simon Magus encounters Philip and tries to buy the Spirit from Peter and John. Evidence: Acts 8:4-25. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/8-4.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:john-the-apostle]] [[person:peter]] [[person:philip-the-evangelist]] [[person:simon-magus]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:jewish-christian]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Samosata is the former capital of Commagene; Christian presence probable via Syrian missions by the mid-1st century. Uncertainty: no direct 1st-century source attests Samosata. Evidence: General inference from proximity to Syrian Christianity. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/place/Samosata\n\nMentions: [[person:unnamed-missionaries]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Scythopolis (Bet She'an) is the largest and most important Decapolis city west of the Jordan, lying at the junction of the Jezreel Valley and the Jordan Valley; it is on the main road from Galilee to Judaea and Jerusalem. Christian presence probable from the earliest Galilean mission. Evidence: General inference. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/place/Beth-Shean\n\nMentions: [[person:unnamed-local-community]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:jewish-christian]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Paul and Barnabas are sent out by the church at Antioch and sail from Seleucia Pieria (the port of Antioch) to Cyprus on the first missionary journey (Acts 13:4); Seleucia Pieria is the harbor gateway to the great Syrian city. Evidence: Acts 13:4. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/13-4.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:barnabas]] [[person:paul]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Sidon is a major Phoenician port; Jesus visits the region of Tyre and Sidon (Mark 7:24-31); early Christian presence probable given its position on the coastal trade route. Uncertainty: no explicit NT founding event at Sidon. Evidence: Mark 7:24. Citations: https://biblehub.com/mark/7-24.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:unnamed-brothers]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Sinope is a major Pontic port city; Christian presence probable given 1 Peter 1:1 addresses Pontus broadly. Uncertainty: no direct 1st-century source attests Sinope specifically. Evidence: 1 Peter 1:1. Citations: https://biblehub.com/1_peter/1-1.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:unnamed-missionaries]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Paul is a native of Tarsus (Acts 9:11; 21:39; 22:3) and returns here after his conversion and Jerusalem visit (Acts 9:30; Gal 1:21). Barnabas retrieves Paul from Tarsus to bring him to Antioch (Acts 11:25). Evidence: Acts 9:30; 11:25-26; Gal 1:21. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/9-30.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:paul-of-tarsus]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Tiberias is the capital built by Herod Antipas c.20 AD on the western shore of the Sea of Galilee, named for Emperor Tiberius; the lake is called 'Sea of Tiberias' in John 6:1 and 21:1; Jesus reportedly avoided the city (built over a cemetery, unclean to Jews), but his ministry centers on its surrounding shores. Christian presence probable from the Galilean mission. Evidence: John 6:1; John 21:1; general records. Citations: https://biblehub.com/john/6-1.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:unnamed-local-community]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:jewish-christian]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Trapezus (modern Trabzon) is the major port of the eastern Black Sea; Georgian/Pontic tradition holds that Andrew the Apostle preached here on his way through Pontus. Uncertainty: Andrew tradition in Pontus is late and unverifiable. Evidence: Later tradition. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/place/Trabzon\n\nMentions: [[person:unnamed-missionaries]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Tradition holds Mark martyred c.68 and Ananianus succeeds; Alexandrian Christianity was initially Jewish. Uncertainty: tradition is post-apostolic and first recorded by Eusebius. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 2.24. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250102.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:ananianus]] [[person:mark-the-evangelist]] [[persuasion:catholic]] [[persuasion:jewish-christian]] [[persuasion:miaphysite]] [[persuasion:orthodox]] [[persuasion:proto-orthodox]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Narbo church attested; as the oldest Roman city in Gaul and capital of the province Narbonensis, Narbo is one of the earliest and most important ecclesiastical centers in Gaul, sitting at the crucial junction of the Via Domitia between Italy and Spain. Citations: https://biblehub.com/titus/3-12.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:unnamed-bishops]] [[persuasion:nicene]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Summary: Ephrem the Syrian is the dominant Syriac theologian; Nisibis will fall to Persia in 363 AD forcing Ephrem to relocate to Edessa. Evidence: Theodoret, Ecc. Hist. 4.29. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Saint-Ephrem-the-Syrian

---

Summary: Nisibis church attested; the School of Nisibis becomes the most important Christian theological institution outside the Greco-Roman world; it produces the Syriac theological tradition that reaches Persia, India, China, and Arabia. Summary: Oxyrhynchus church attested; the site is the single most productive source of early Christian papyri ever discovered — over 500,000 fragments including NT texts, apocryphal gospels, liturgical documents, and private Christian letters, making it unparalleled for understanding grassroots early Christianity. Summary: Petra church attested; later a bishop of Petra is known from 5th century conciliar lists. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2602.htm\n\nMentions: [[person:unnamed-bishops]] [[persuasion:nicene]] [[polity:roman-empire]]

Evidence: Prudentius, Peristephanon 5; Augustine, Serm. 274.
